content.full_text:
Ravi Mehta
Chairman

Fair Political Practices Commission

December 31, 1996

Robert W. Ford
Chair
CDF Firefighters PAC
924 Enterprise Drive
Sacramento, California 95825

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-351

Dear Mr. Ford:

This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

You are the chair of a general purpose recipient committee that makes contributions to
other committees to support or oppose local initiatives. You would like to know how
Proposition 208 will affect that activity.

CONCLUSION

Proposition 208, which amended the Act and goes into effect on January 1, 1997, has no
provisions that would restrict your committee's ability to make contributions to committees
established for local ballot measures.

If your committee makes contributions to candidates, the initiative could impact your
committee in other ways. Contributions from your committee to candidates or their controlled
committees are subject to contribution limits. (Section 85301.) Also, Proposition 208 provides
in Section 85311 that "all payments made by a person established, financed, maintained, or
controlled by any business entity, labor organization, association, political party, or any other
person or group of such persons shall be considered to be made by a single person." Therefore, if

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18000 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-96-351
Page 2

your committee is "established, financed, maintained, or controlled" by another organization,
then any contributions your committee makes will be aggregated with the contributions of the
affiliated organizations for the purposes of contribution limits to candidates and political parties.

If your committee makes contributions to candidates, the committee may not accept
contributions from any persons totaling more than $500 per year. (Section 85301(d).) If your
organization has more than 100 members, receives no more than $50.00 in contributions from
any person, and has been in existence for more than six months, the committee may qualify as a
small contributor committee and thus be permitted to give higher contributions to candidates.
(Sections 85203, 85302.) Finally, if your committee makes independent expenditures of more
than $1,000 in support or opposition to any candidate, the committee may not accept a
contribution in excess of $250. (Section 85301.) I have enclosed a chart summarizing
Proposition 208, as well as a copy of the initiative itself for your convenience. If you have any
other questions regarding Proposition 208, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Liane Randolph
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:LR:ak

Enclosures
